source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 15.3.2 SetUID (pp. 291-293)

options: While an executable runs, its executor temporarily gains the identity of the file's OWNER; only valid on executable binaries
It makes a file readable by everyone
It locks a file against deletion
It sets the default group for new files in a directory
questions.why: SUID on an executable makes it run with the owner's identity for the duration; the identity reverts when it finishes.

options: It must write /etc/shadow (permissions 000), so it temporarily runs as root, the file's owner
Because ordinary users cannot run it otherwise
questions.why: shadow is 000, so a normal user's passwd run temporarily becomes root (the owner) to update it.

questions.q: Add the SetUID permission to the executable myprog (symbolic form).
questions.why: chmod u+s sets SUID; numerically it is the leading 4 (e.g. chmod 4755).

questions.q: Which leading octal digit represents SUID?
In ls -l you see a capital 'S' in the owner's execute position (e.g. -rwSr--r--). What does that mean?
options: SUID is set but the owner lacks execute (x), so the SUID cannot actually be used
questions.why: A capital S means SUID is set on a file whose owner has no x - so it is non-functional (a lowercase s means x is present).

questions.q: Find all SUID files on the system and long-list them.
questions.why: -perm -4000 matches files with the SUID bit; a good habit for auditing since SUID can escalate to root.

questions.q: Remove the SetUID permission from myprog (symbolic form).
